aliases: Fried pastry with spiced potato filling

english: Fried pastry with spiced potato filling

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for samosa; the food is adequately represented by FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of vegetable samosa consists of two pieces, each approximately 60 g, totalling around 120 g.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa, vegetable — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken from the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods table for vegetable samosa (potato-pea filling, deep-fried). This is the primary approved regional source for Indian dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for vegetable samosa was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food: fried wheat-flour pastry with potato filling, consistent with values reported for similar South Asian fried snacks in Atkinson et al. (2021) and peer-reviewed literature. Potato-filled fried pastry GI values cluster in the 50–60 range; 55 is used as the central defensible estimate. GL per serving calculated as (55 × 29.5 × 1.20) / 100 = 19.5.

hypertension.guidance: A 120 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 372 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit samosa intake to one piece and avoid high-sodium chutneys to keep total meal sodium within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (55) and significant carbohydrate load (29.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 55 and a glycemic load of approximately 19.5 per standard serving, vegetable samosa can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; people with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to one piece and pair with a low-GI accompaniment such as plain yoghurt rather than sweet chutney.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat (14.8 g per 100 g) from deep-frying, with saturated fat at 2.1 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying contributes significant total fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat vegetable samosa as an occasional snack, preferring baked or air-fried versions where available, and limiting frequency to reduce cumulative saturated fat and energy intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable samosa is a classic South Asian deep-fried pastry snack consisting of a crispy, triangular wheat-flour shell encasing a spiced filling of mashed potato, green peas, onion, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and green chilli. The outer shell is made from maida (refined wheat flour) mixed with oil or ghee and water, rolled thin and folded into a cone before being filled and sealed. The samosa is then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crunchy. The texture contrast between the shatteringly crisp pastry and the soft, aromatic filling is a defining characteristic. Vegetable samosa is widely consumed across India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and the South Asian diaspora in Singapore and Southeast Asia. It is commonly served as a street food, tea-time snack, or starter, accompanied by mint-coriander chutney or tamarind chutney. Nutritionally, a 120 g serving provides approximately 314 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 17.8 g fat, and 35.4 g carbohydrate. The sodium content of around 372 mg per serving warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low category), but the glycemic load per serving is approximately 19.5, which is clinically meaningful for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The fat content is primarily from deep-frying and is predominantly unsaturated. Vegetable samosa is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is typically halal-compliant. It contains gluten and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a vegetable samosa?
What is the GI of samosa?
How much fat is in a samosa?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), which is the primary approved regional source for Indian dishes and provides an exact match for vegetable samosa. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021) as no direct GI measurement for vegetable samosa was found in the University of Sydney GI Database; confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food item. 2. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food item. 3. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 for this vegan food item; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN as the filling and pastry contain no animal-derived ingredients in the standard vegetable formulation. 4. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0; naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for higher confidence. 6. The restaurant serving (Mr Tandoor, Boat Quay, Singapore) is priced at SGD 9.90 for what is likely a multi-piece portion; serving size of 120 g (2 pieces) is used as a standard estimate. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for comparable fried wheat-flour pastry with potato filling, as ICMR-NIN does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this item. 8. glycemic_index set to 55 (low category per ≤55 threshold); gi_category is low.
